skill.name: medical-research-algorithm-matcher
skill.description: Matches a user's biomedical research direction, disease problem, study aim, data modality, and resource constraints to the most relevant recent algorithms and method papers. Always search real recent algorithm literature first, prioritize the last 12 months, expand to 1–3 years only when needed, and add canonical baselines only when necessary. Every formal algorithm recommendation must include the verified primary method paper, plus published downstream papers that actually cite or use the algorithm when such papers are found, with DOI when available. Never fabricate papers, algorithm names, authors, journals, years, DOI, PMID, links, or benchmark claims. If no directly verified algorithm paper is found, say so explicitly.
skill.summary: Match your biomedical research direction to verified, recent algorithm papers with structured literature-backed recommendations.
skill.author: aipoch

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced 17 flagged patterns with a computed score of 100/100; upon manual review all findings are false positives except one benign documentation URL. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags were triggered by the text 'md' appearing in Markdown file extensions and method abbreviations, not actual cryptographic code. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags matched Markdown inline code spans used to describe input format templates, not shell execution. The 'network/system reconnaissance' flags matched ordinary prose in documentation files. The single hardcoded URL is a benign GitHub source reference link. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or actual security risk was identified.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub Source URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 7 contains a hardcoded URL pointing to the skill author's GitHub repository as a documentation source attribution. This is a benign reference link and does not represent a network request, data exfiltration, or access to untrusted resources. The URL is https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills, which is the published source of the skill. Confidence: 0.10. Confidence reasoning: The URL is a static documentation attribution in a Markdown blockquote; no code path executes or fetches this URL at runtime.

content.user_title: Match Your Biomedical Research to Verified Algorithms
content.value_statement: Finding recent, verified algorithms for a specific biomedical research direction is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill matches your disease, data type, and study goal to a structured shortlist of real algorithm papers with DOI, verified citations, and an honest statement of what could not be confirmed.
actual_capabilities: Match a research direction, disease, and data modality to the most relevant algorithm families
Retrieve and present verified recent algorithm papers with DOI and direct links
Distinguish baseline comparator methods, primary recent matches, and frontier upgrade options
Explicitly flag algorithm names that cannot be directly verified in the literature
Include downstream use papers to assess real-world directional fit beyond technical plausibility
Provide a structured benchmarking and comparison plan for the recommended method stack
limitations: Cannot access papers behind paywalls; verification depends on publicly reachable sources such as PubMed, arXiv, and publisher DOI pages
Does not generate, execute, or debug implementation code for the recommended algorithms
Recommendations are not suitable for clinical treatment decisions or patient care guidance
Algorithm literature coverage is bounded by training data and publicly indexed sources; very new or obscure papers may not be verifiable

use_cases.title: Biomedical PhD Student Choosing Methods
use_cases.description: A graduate student working on gastric precancerous lesions needs to select pathway-aware algorithms for a multi-omics classification project. The skill identifies relevant algorithm families, retrieves recent papers from the last 12 months, and presents a baseline-to-frontier recommendation stack with verified DOI.

use_cases.title: Bioinformatician Evaluating Named Algorithms
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician has heard of specific algorithm names such as PathHDNN and wants to know whether they are real, recent, and directionally appropriate before committing to implementation. The skill verifies each named algorithm against published literature and explicitly marks any that cannot be confirmed.

use_cases.title: Principal Investigator Updating a Research Protocol
use_cases.description: A PI running an established DEG plus PPI plus Cox workflow wants to know what newer algorithms have emerged in the last year that could improve their immunotherapy-response prediction pipeline. The skill identifies upgrade options, compares them against the existing baseline, and explains what each adds.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Research Direction Match
prompt_templates.prompt: I am studying [disease or topic]. My research goal is [goal]. What are the most relevant recent algorithms I should consider?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: user has a disease and goal but no specific algorithm in mind

prompt_templates.title: Data Modality and Goal Match
prompt_templates.prompt: My project involves [disease or topic] using [data modality such as scRNA-seq or multi-omics]. I want to [analysis goal such as classify cell states or predict treatment response]. Match me to recent algorithms that fit this setup.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: user specifies data type and analysis objective

prompt_templates.title: Named Algorithm Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: I have heard of the following algorithms: [algorithm name 1], [algorithm name 2]. Are these real, recent, and relevant to my project on [disease] with [data type]? Verify each one and tell me if any cannot be confirmed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: user wants specific named algorithms checked

prompt_templates.title: Workflow Upgrade Request
prompt_templates.prompt: My current workflow is [existing method stack such as DEG plus PPI plus Cox]. I work on [disease or topic] with [data modality]. Find the latest algorithms from the last 12 months that could meaningfully upgrade this workflow, and explain what each one adds over my current approach.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: user has an existing pipeline and wants targeted recent upgrades

output_examples.input: Gastric precancerous lesions. Recommend recent pathway-aware algorithms for early intervention target discovery.
output_examples.output: Research direction interpreted as: pathway-guided predictive modeling for early-stage gastric cancer intervention targets. Algorithm need classified as: pathway-guided interpretable modeling plus disease-gene prioritization. Recommended stack: Baseline — standard GSEA and Cox regression. Primary recent match — a pathway-aware graph neural network method published within 12 months with verified DOI and downstream application papers in gastric cancer contexts. Frontier upgrade — multi-omics integration method with translational stratification capability. Each recommendation includes the primary method paper, first author, year, venue, DOI, and at least one downstream use paper where found. Methods that could not be verified against published sources are listed separately with explicit statements.

output_examples.input: I want to verify whether PathHDNN and HiDDEN are real and relevant for immunotherapy-response prediction in lung cancer.
output_examples.output: PathHDNN: verification attempted against PubMed, arXiv, and publisher DOI sources. Result and fit assessment provided if a verified primary method paper is found. HiDDEN: No directly verified algorithm paper identified yet for this method name in the current biomedical scope. The name is noted but not promoted to a formal recommendation. Nearest verified alternatives for immunotherapy-response prediction in lung cancer are provided with full literature evidence.

best_practices: Provide both the disease or phenotype and the specific research goal when making a request; the more precisely you state what you are trying to solve, the more targeted the algorithm match will be.
If you have a current method stack, describe it explicitly so the skill can identify meaningful upgrades rather than listing all possible recent methods.
Treat the verified literature pack as the primary output: check each DOI independently before committing to an algorithm, as publication landscapes change quickly.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the skill to recommend algorithms without stating a disease, topic, or research goal; a generic request produces only broad algorithm families without directional relevance.
Do not assume a named algorithm is verified simply because it appears in the output; always check the explicit verification status shown for each method name.
Do not use this skill as a substitute for full systematic literature review; it provides a matched shortlist and starting evidence pack, not exhaustive coverage of all published methods.

faq.question: What does it mean when the skill says a method is not directly verified?
faq.answer: It means that a primary method paper for that algorithm name could not be confirmed through an accessible source such as PubMed, DOI landing page, arXiv, or publisher page. The name is noted but not promoted to a formal recommendation. Nearest verified alternatives are provided instead.

faq.question: Can this skill access the full text of papers?
faq.answer: No. The skill relies on indexed metadata, abstracts, and publicly accessible pages. Papers behind paywalls may not be fully verifiable. When a DOI cannot be confirmed, the skill states this explicitly.

faq.question: Does the skill generate code for the recommended algorithms?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on method selection and literature verification. It does not write, execute, or debug implementation code. It can describe what an algorithm does and where its original code repository may be found if that information is in the public record.

faq.question: How recent are the algorithm recommendations?
faq.answer: The skill prioritizes papers from the last 12 months first, then expands to 1 to 3 years if recent directly relevant papers are sparse. Canonical baselines are added only when needed for fair comparison. The recency boundary is always stated explicitly in the output.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for clinical or treatment decisions?
faq.answer: No. This skill is for research method selection only. It is not designed for and must not be used to inform patient care, clinical treatment choices, or diagnostic decisions.

faq.question: What should I do if the skill says recent literature is weak for my direction?
faq.answer: The skill will state this clearly and explain the nearest validated alternatives. This is an honest signal that the field may not yet have mature recent methods for your exact problem. The output will include the best available options with their limitations noted.
